title: How this was built
lead: A short, honest note on building this site, and what it has to do with the eval work.

sections.body_html: This is a small, free place to learn how modern AI works: 65 lessons across five tracks, twelve interactive labs, quizzes, and flashcards, all served as a static site. The lesson count is not the interesting part. The interesting part is that I pointed the same honesty discipline I use on research at the product's own quality, and it kept catching me.

sections.heading: The tell
sections.body_html:
A reader noticed that on the quizzes, the correct answer was usually the longest option. I measured it: across all 199 quiz and exercise items, the correct answer was the longest one 98% of the time. Random would be 25%. You could pass every quiz on the site without reading a single question.

That is a measurement problem before it is a content problem, so I treated it like one.

sections.heading: Doer is not grader
sections.body_html:
I rewrote the throwaway distractors ("It never makes mistakes") into plausible misconceptions matched in length to the correct answer, then checked the result the way I check an experiment: with a different model, blind to the answer key. It was asked to pick the best option itself and flag any other option it found defensible. Where it disagreed with the key, I had a card to review.

It earned its place. It caught six distractors that were not merely plausible but accidentally true: "average more scores to reduce judge noise" really is a noise-reduction technique; "do it yourself with no AI" really is fine for a small edit you want to learn from. Plausible-but-wrong is easy. Plausible-but-actually-correct is the failure that slips through, and a grader that shares the author's blind spots waves it through. A decorrelated one does not. It is the same reason the eval harness uses a judge from a different family than the doer.

sections.heading: Catching my own overcorrection
sections.body_html: Fixing the length tell introduced a second one: my new distractors leaned on absolute words (always, never, guarantees) far more than the correct answers did, a 20-point skew that rewards "avoid the absolute-sounding option." I over-corrected the fix to zero, which is itself a tell in reverse, since now the absolute option is always correct. The honest target was neither extreme: match the rate so the feature carries no signal either way. Writing down that I overshot is cheaper than pretending the first pass was right.

sections.heading: Making honesty the default, not the heroics
sections.body_html: Three properties now have to hold for any future lesson, checked deterministically on every commit and in CI: the correct answer is not usually the longest, absolute language does not predict the answer, and answer position stays even. The labs carry the same discipline in plain sight: every widget states what it is, and every illustrative one says so ("a hand-placed map, not extracted from a real model"). A learning tool that is loose with its own claims is not in a position to teach calibration.

sections.heading: Why this is the artifact
sections.body_html: The through-line is the one I care about most: do not fool yourself when you measure, especially when you are the one being measured. Decorrelate the doer and the grader. Ship the contradiction you find, including your own. I find it more convincing to show that discipline running quietly inside a real, shipped product than to assert it in the abstract. This is where the eval thinking and the building meet.
